route plan: through colomos park, the loop along the camellones, around the small pond where the herons live, back to mom's house. 8.4 km by garmin's last measure of this loop in 2019. i ran it as a high schooler in the rain at 14, ran it as a college freshman in 95° heat at 19, ran it pre-cdmx-move at 28. there is a layer of geography here that is also a layer of self-history. running it tuesday morning at 33 with mom waiting on a CT result is going to feel like every previous version of me is also running it.

pre-run check: shoes laced, phone with apple maps and garmin chest strap, water bottle, cap, sunscreen on the back of the neck. mom said 'cuídate del sol, mijo' and 'no te tardes' as i left, which is the same exact two sentences she said when i was 14. some templates don't update.

actually-running update — wrote this in my head for the last 2km, putting it down now: i finished in 39:27, which is slower than my cdmx 8k pace by about 90 seconds per km, which is the heat tax. heart rate average 168, peak 184, which felt fine because the run wasn't about fitness. the camellones smelled like jasmine. the pond herons are still there. i passed two viejitos walking and they nodded. i passed a kid on a bike who looked exactly like me at twelve. i did not cry on the run because if i'd started i wouldn't have stopped. i did sit on a bench at km 6 for ninety seconds and stared at the sky. the sky in zapopan in october at 9am is so blue it looks photoshopped. i remember now why i used to run here. i used to run here to be reminded that the world is bigger than the thing in front of me. that still works. small wins. heading to shower.

— the funny thing about being on the porch with both parents at once is that dad does most of the talking and mom does most of the looking. dad describes things, mom watches them. dad will tell you which neighbor's car broke down last week; mom will look at the neighbor's car and tell you the neighbor is sad. the division of labor in zapopan is consistent and unspoken and beautiful. they've been doing this for 40 years and they're not going to stop.

— after the porch hours we went inside and watched the late news together. there was a story about la polar (cdmx) being investigated for a different licensing matter. dad laughed and said 'tu cantina está en las noticias, mijo'. i did not engage. some battles you don't bring to your parents.

— the porch hours have a thing called 'el silencio de papa' which is a family in-joke about how dad will sit on the porch for hours without saying anything and then suddenly emit a single declarative statement. tonight's: 'el zacate está creciendo, hay que cortarlo'. silence for 23 minutes. then: 'la mate, ¿cuándo te volvieron a aumentar la renta?'. so he was tracking the rent thing this whole time. he hadn't asked because he wanted me to bring it up, and i hadn't, so on the porch in the silence he found a moment to ask without making a thing of it. dad's emotional architecture is mostly silence with single declarative sentences emerging from it.

ran 8k in colomos at 9am, came back, drank water, took a cold shower, helped mom hang laundry. it is now 11:30am. there are still 4.5 hours until 'by 4pm'. mom is in the garden. dad is reading a physics paper i didn't know he still got delivered. i'm going to write code for an hour because if i don't have a screen in front of me i'm going to start cleaning closets. update: roberto pinged me about a 'small clarification' on routeopt. it was not small. i answered anyway. it took my mind off the clock for forty minutes. kind of miraculous. give him this one.

specifically he asked me to validate that the memory fix i shipped to staging last week was 'production-grade'. i said yes, walked him through the test i added, walked him through the load profile, walked him through the abort semantics. he said 'great, thanks, this is exactly the kind of write-up i need from senior engineers'. i would normally roll my eyes at 'exactly the kind of write-up i need' but today, today specifically, the validation hit a different way. i'll take it.

what i actually wrote him, since he asked for production-grade evidence: (1) the unit test i added (paste of the timeout_aborts_sibling_task test), (2) heaptrack output before-and-after with rss curves, (3) the locust output showing flat memory over 90 minutes at production-shape load, (4) a note about the runtime sizing concern that's still open and a recommended sequence for resolving it (load test bumped to 2x prod traffic, observe burst patterns, decide on multi_thread runtime). that was a 1500-word writeup typed in 35 minutes. i don't think i could have produced it that fast last month. zapopan operating system, apparently, is also better at concise technical writing.

mom watered the garden plants while i was typing. dad finished the physics paper, started reading a chess book — 'el final de torre' by something. he said 'mira esta posición' and showed me a king-and-rook endgame on his pocket chess board. white to move, win in 12. i looked at it for a minute, said 'la torre detrás del peón pasado'. he said 'sí, exacto'. proud-dad face. that lasted ten seconds. then he closed the book and stared out the window. dad's stare-out-the-window is its own state — he's not seeing anything, he's running the same loop i'm running, he's waiting for the phone to ring. we are all in the same nervous system right now. the house is one big breath being held.

12:45pm now. five and a quarter hours until 'by 4pm'. i ate two corn tortillas and some refried beans because mom said 'come algo' and at 33 you still listen when your mom says come algo. nube on whatsapp video earlier — camila held her up, she looked confused at the camera, then licked it. that helped. ten minutes of nube licking the screen would help anything. the day is being crossed off in micro-units.

mateo called me at 11pm tijuana time. asked how mom was. asked how dad was. asked how i was. i said 'ok, llorando un poco'. he said 'normal, hermano'. then he said 'sé que el doctor beltrán es bueno. el papá de un amigo mío en la base militar pasó por él el año pasado, mismo tipo de bulto, fue un quiste. quédate con eso esta noche'. i did. i stayed with it. quistic. probabilistically a quiste. i fell asleep around 2am holding that word.

— i logged a checkout note from the day: ate three meals, slept ten hours, ran twice (gentle), did 20 minutes of pull-ups on the door frame, read 40 pages, did not check brújula slack, called camila twice, called nube three times via camila, played chess with dad and lost twice. that's the full ledger. not bad for a sunday.

— at dinner, abuela elsa (mom's older sister, 78) called from puerto vallarta to ask about the CT. she said 'mija, dile a sebastiansito que no se preocupe, que la familia castellanos siempre ha tenido la cabeza dura' (the family has always had a hard head). it was meant to be funny. mom laughed. abuela elsa sounded older than i remember. mom thanked her and they hung up. mom said 'a tu tía abuela también la queremos mucho, pero ya está mayor'. yes. ya está mayor. everyone is.

1pm. nothing. lunch. ate without tasting. mom is calmer than me, which is humbling. she made sopa de fideo because that's what she makes when she's nervous, it's the kind of food that requires you to babysit a pot for thirty minutes which is its own form of meditation. dad ate two bowls. i ate one and pushed the noodles around. mom looked at my plate and didn't say anything. that's how i know she knows. usually she'd comment. today she let me have it.

she also did the thing where she sets out a little plate of avocado on the side because i mentioned three years ago that i missed having avocado with sopa de fideo, and she has remembered every visit since. mexican mothers do that. they collect your offhand preferences and store them indefinitely. it's a love language i am extremely bad at reciprocating. i need to be better.

inventory of avocado-equivalents from my mom's memory bank, things i've mentioned once and she has stored forever:
— i don't like cilantro (soap gene), so she chops it for dad's plate but not mine. for years.
— i prefer corn tortillas to flour. no flour ever appears on my plate. mateo gets flour. parents have parallel plates.
— i drink yerba mate in the morning and coffee in the afternoon. she's stocked yerba in the kitchen since 2019 even though nobody in zapopan drinks yerba except me.
— i like my coffee unsweetened. dad takes two sugars. she makes mine separately. the same little glass next to the same red mug.
— i like a dash of lime in beans, mateo doesn't. she puts a lime quarter on the side of my bowl. she has done this since i was 17.
— i'm vegetarian since 2022. she has mastered three vegetarian meals — chiles rellenos with cheese, sopa de fideo, and a vegetarian albondigas — and rotates them in. she still puts meat on dad's plate.

each of those is a small piece of attention sustained over years. i don't track that for her. i don't even know what avocado-equivalent she has — what offhand preference she once mentioned that i could have stored and reciprocated. let me think. she likes the flowers from the corner stall on av. patria. she takes lemon in her water but not lime. she prefers the gel-tip pens to ballpoints. she likes the bread from the panadería on calle hidalgo over doña marta's (which is a treason i don't tell doña marta about). she watches the news at 7pm but mutes the politics segment. she keeps a small box of postcards she's saved from places mateo and i have traveled. she's never asked us to send them. she just keeps the ones we send.

i'm going to start sending her postcards again. from cdmx. from anywhere. just a postcard a month. write a few sentences. tell her something. she'll keep them. the box will grow. that's what you do for the person who has been remembering avocado for three years.

still 4pm-minus-three-hours. her phone face-up on the kitchen counter. she keeps glancing at it. so does dad. so do i. the phone is winning the staring contest with all three of us. we are very bad at this kind of waiting. mexican families are dramatic in big moments and stoic in waiting moments and we have lots of practice with the first kind and almost none with the second.

— camila's voice notes today were great. she sent a 9-second one with nube purring directly into the phone speaker, no commentary, just nube. that's exactly the right response to a sister-in-law-in-distress. no advice. just cat audio. camila is good at this stuff in ways i am not. i'm going to remember to thank her properly when i'm back.

— mom said something interesting at lunch: 'mijo, el doctor beltrán dijo que si el resultado tarda dos días puede ser por la cola del laboratorio, no por el resultado en sí'. so the lab queue, not the finding, drives the delay. that's the explanation she's holding onto and i'm holding onto it with her.

— camila sent a video of nube watching a moth on the patio. nube was making the chittering noise that means 'hunt mode'. moth survived. 23 seconds of cat content. exactly what was needed.

2:45pm. phone is on the table. we're all in the living room. mom has the phone closer to her, dad in his chair, me on the couch, mateo on whatsapp video on the laptop next to me waving from his apartment in tijuana, camila just joined from cdmx with nube on her lap. nube is licking her face on screen. mom keeps glancing at the phone like it might bite. dad opened a spanish wine an hour ago which is either a celebration plan or a coping plan. i don't know which yet. i'll know in 75 minutes.

mateo brought a beer to the call which is the most mateo thing possible — he set it on his desk like he's at a sports bar waiting for kickoff. camila is in our cdmx kitchen and i can see the calendar on the wall behind her with a blue dot for our barcelona trip in march. i love that she joined the call. she didn't have to. i didn't ask. she just opened the laptop at 2:30 her time and waited.

she's wearing the green sweater i got her last christmas — which she only wears when she's nervous, like it's a weighted blanket disguised as a sweater. she said 'hola, isabela' to mom and mom waved and tried to smile. mateo said 'hola jefa, tranquila' and mom said 'tranquila tú, hijo'. dad raised his wine glass at the camera and mateo raised his beer. the family ritual of waiting through a frightening thing, distributed across four cities (cdmx, gdl, tj, são paulo if hugo joins) and one couch. i told hugo sr he didn't have to join, the three-hour time difference is rough; he texted me 'estoy con ustedes en pensamiento, abrazos' which is hugo for 'i love you all, im here'.

camila brought her chiang book to the camera so i could see it — she's reading the same one i'm reading because she said 'si lo lees lento, lo leo lento contigo'. i didn't know she was doing that. she's been reading at the same pace as me, on her side of the apartment, since i started in september. she's at page 162. i'm at page 184. she just bumped up to my page count this morning by reading on the metro. i didn't know any of this. i'm finding out by glimpsing the bookmark sticking out of her copy on a video call.

mom keeps touching her own neck. not the lump side, the other side. unconscious. dad is holding the wine glass without drinking. i'm watching the phone. mateo is watching the phone through the camera. nube fell asleep on camila's lap. it is 3:01pm. one more hour. fifty-nine minutes. mom said 'cuéntenme algo, distráiganme'. mateo started a story about sofía and the wedding cake tasting. it filled twelve minutes. nobody remembers the details. we just needed the noise.

— at the dinner with the family last night, dad asked me about the rust migration. i was surprised. he doesn't usually ask about work. i tried to explain it in non-technical terms — 'the system that figures out the best route for a delivery truck, but in a faster language' — and he said 'a ver si no la rompes' and i laughed and said 'estoy tratando de no romperla, pa'. he said 'tú eres el que la va a arreglar si la rompes, así que está bien'. that was a kind thing to say.

— at one point during the week i caught myself talking out loud to nube as if she were here. dad walked past and didn't comment. mom walked past and said 'ya, ya, mijo, ya casi vuelves'. she knew exactly what i was doing. she always does.

— last note: i did 60 pushups before bed because the body needed something to do. i was way too jittery from the wait. brown belt me said 'do something physical'. i did. it helped a little. then i couldn't sleep for an hour anyway.

— mom slipped me 500 pesos at breakfast this morning 'para tu camino'. i did not need it. she has been doing this since i was 18. i took it and put it in my wallet. she felt better. i felt 8 years old again. fair trade.

3:20pm. forty minutes. i'm going to put down the laptop because if i keep typing i'll have written a novel by the time the call comes. one thought before i go quiet: i was thinking about abuela lupita earlier and what i regret about her — not visiting her last year, the way i waited too long because work, because cdmx, because the assumption that she'd always be there. mom is 62 and healthy and right here in the kitchen and i am sitting in her living room because i flew here on 24-hour notice and i am not going to make the abuela mistake twice. whatever the call says, i'm here, i was here, i'll be here for whatever comes next. the rest is the rest.

the specific guilt about abuela has been sitting in me for seven years and it doesn't really get smaller, it just changes shape. some weeks it's 'i should have visited more often when she was still mentally there', other weeks it's 'i should have called every sunday for the last year', other weeks it's 'i should have flown back when mom said estable because mom was wrong'. tonight whatever shape it takes is the right shape because tonight it's the engine that got me on the plane on thursday.

abuela lupita is also the person who gave me the bombilla mate cup with my name on it. i drank yerba this morning out of that cup, here in the house, with abuela's name engraved on the rim seen by mom in the kitchen and unmentioned. i'm sure mom saw it. she always sees it. she has never said anything about it because she also has feelings about her mother dying and us not being there and that's its own private channel between us that we don't broadcast on. but the cup is on the kitchen counter, like a small altar, and it has been part of every yerba i've drunk in this house since 2017. mom has never moved it.

other people i'm going to do better with after this week, even if mom is fine: dad. mateo. hugo sr. camila's siblings. pablo's parents who i've met twice and never followed up with. profe esteban. carla — when she's back from leave i'm taking her for coffee. i'm going to send mom a postcard a month. i'm going to call dad once a week instead of once every three weeks. i'm going to fly to gdl twice a year minimum even when there's no medical reason. i'm going to fly to tj for mateo's wedding and stay an extra two days. i'm going to take camila to são paulo to see hugo at least every six months. these are not aspirations. these are the operational consequences of seeing your mom touch her own neck while waiting for a phone call.

ok 38 minutes. stopping here. closing laptop. brb.

— small thing i logged in my notes app: the kitchen calendar's 'CT, viernes 8am' was still there saturday morning even though the appointment was already done. mom hadn't crossed it out. she was waiting until results to cross it out. that's the kind of small psychic detail that shows up in a journal entry six months from now and breaks me.

— small log: dad asked me what brújula stock was worth (we have an option pool). i said 'pa, son options, no son acciones todavía'. he said 'pero si las pueden vender'. i said 'sí, en una salida'. he said 'pero si no hay salida'. i said 'entonces no'. he nodded. dad understands risk. he understood it before i did.

— before bed i checked the brújula slack briefly. roberto had posted a 700-word announcement about 'standup hygiene improvements' which i read but did not engage with. it was the kind of post that wants reactions to validate it. the team had given it three thumbs-ups. that's the maximum i'd give it too. some battles you fight by NOT clicking the heart.

— mom asked me 'cuándo es el próximo viaje a guadalajara, mijo?' i said 'navidad'. she said 'de verdad?' i said 'de verdad, ma'. that's six weeks. she nodded. she is counting weeks. so am i.

phone just rang. it's 3:47pm. mom picking up. i can't breathe. brb. dad froze with the wine glass mid-air. mateo on screen put down his beer. camila put both hands on nube. mom said 'sí, doctor beltrán'. that's all i caught before brain went quiet. typing this with one thumb behind the laptop where mom can't see me being weird. heart in my throat. both hands shaking. apparently brown belt training does not prepare you for this kind of round. brb for real.

ok. dr beltrán was still on the phone with mom for like twelve minutes when i started this. i can hear her saying 'sí doctor', 'sí doctor', 'gracias', and she is nodding, and i don't know what's being said but the nodding is steady, not panicked. dad is gripping the arm of his chair. mateo is silent on video. camila is silent on video. nube is asleep on screen, oblivious. mom just said 'entonces no es...' and then nodded. i don't know what's after the 'no es'. i'm afraid to interpret. i'm not breathing well. brb again.

— ok update mom said 'sin invasión' just now and i had to grab the table to not fall over. 'sin invasión'. that means it isn't spreading. that means it's encapsulated. that means — i can't say what it means yet. she's still on the call. she's still nodding. dad has tears in his eyes already and the call isn't even over. brb.

— second update. she's writing things down on the notepad she keeps by the phone. her hand is steady. that's a tell. mom's hand shakes when she's bracing for bad news. her hand is steady. i'm reading her face which is, for the first time in seven days, not braced. her shoulders are lower. dad has not unfrozen but his eyes are tracking her. mateo is leaning into the camera. camila is biting her thumbnail. nube has rolled over and exposed her belly. nube is the only one in this household with the right energy for this moment.

— third update. she said 'biopsia de aguja fina, viernes próximo, ambulatoria'. that's the standard FNA biopsy procedure for confirmation. fine-needle aspiration. ambulatory means she goes home the same day. that's the workflow for a 'we're 95% sure it's benign, just need histology to close the file' diagnosis. that's not the workflow for cancer. cancer goes to surgical biopsy or excisional biopsy, not FNA. FNA is the chill version. FNA is 'come in tuesday, take 30 minutes, results in a week'. i learned this distinction at 1am tuesday from googling that i told myself i wasn't going to do.

— fourth update. she said 'bueno doctor, muchísimas gracias' which is end-of-call energy. she's wrapping up. she's about to hang up. dad is leaning forward. mateo's hand is hovering over his beer. camila's eyes are full. i'm trying to type and breathe at the same time. she said 'sí doctor, hasta el viernes'. and then she said 'gracias, gracias, hasta luego'. and then she put the phone down. and the room went very quiet. brb to the next message because i need to actually be in the room for what happens next.

— camila and i had our first argument in two weeks last night, and it was about my flight back. i said 'i'll book wednesday'. she said 'don't, stay until friday'. i said 'i need to be home'. she said 'your home will still be here on friday. your mom's house won't always'. i hung up to think. i called back fifteen minutes later and said 'ok, friday'. she said 'ok'. that's how arguments work in this relationship — short fuse, fast reset, real outcome.

— while i was packing for the flight back, mom came in with a tupperware of frijoles de la olla and a bag of conchas. 'para cami'. for camila. she was sending food across the country because that's what she does. i said 'ma, no me dejan pasar comida en el avión'. she said 'sí te dejan, en el equipaje documentado'. i checked. she was right. she's always right about logistics. the conchas made it back to cdmx.

— before sleep mom came to my room and asked if she could lay down on the bed for a minute. she lay down on the bed. she didn't say anything. she closed her eyes. i sat on the edge and didn't say anything either. after twelve minutes she said 'ya, ya estoy bien, gracias mijo'. she got up and went to her room. that was its own kind of mom-conversation.

— last log of the night before bed: my phone battery says 73%. mom is asleep. dad is asleep. nube is purring through camila's whatsapp voice memo. yerba mate is cold. tomorrow is tuesday. i love them all. closing.

she hung up. she put the phone face-down on the table. she said 'el doctor dice que es muy probable un lipoma. necesita una biopsia con aguja para confirmar al cien, pero la imagen se ve consistente con tejido graso, sin nodos sospechosos, sin invasión'. i'm typing this verbatim because i don't trust my translation right now. then she said 'tengo que ir el viernes para la biopsia, pero el doctor no está preocupado'. dad stood up and hugged her. mateo on video burst into tears. camila on video burst into tears. i sat down on the floor because my legs went. nube woke up because of camila's sniffling and started licking her face again on screen. mom is okay. probably. pending biopsy. but okay. dad opened the spanish wine. mateo opened a beer in tijuana on video. camila and i clinked nothing across screens. i'll process all of this properly later. right now the only thing i can write is: she's okay. she's okay. she's okay.

three hours later, after dinner: we ate. mom ate. dad ate three plates because he hadn't eaten properly in five days. mateo stayed on video while we ate, like he was sitting at the table from tijuana. camila waved goodbye around 8pm because she had a foster + partners early call. i extended my flight by two days — flying back saturday instead of thursday — so i can be here through the FNA on friday. roberto okayed it in slack within five minutes. daniela texted 'gracias por avisarme, mucha tranquilidad para tu mamá'. pablo sent a voice note that just said 'cabrón, llóralo, llóralo bien, te abrazo desde acá'.

mom went to bed early. dad and i sat on the porch and didn't talk for an hour. then he said 'hijo, gracias por haber venido' and i said 'no era opción no venir, pa' and he said 'ya sé, pero gracias' and we sat for another twenty minutes and he went to bed. i'm still on the porch. it's almost midnight. the cicadas are loud. the lemon tree dad planted in 2008 is doing its thing. i can see a single light on across the street, somebody else awake at midnight, somebody else's small private moment of relief or terror or just insomnia. zapopan is full of those tonight, like every night.

i'm going to sleep. but before i do — small log for future-me, when this week is a year ago and i've forgotten how it felt: it felt like the floor came back. for seven days the floor was somewhere else, and at 3:47pm on tuesday it came back. friday's biopsy is friday's problem. mom is okay. probably. pending histology. but the floor is back. that's what i needed to write down before sleep tonight.

— last log of the night before sleep: it's 11:30pm zapopan time. mom is asleep. dad is asleep in his chair. nube would be on my chest if she were here. she's not. camila is in cdmx. tomorrow is the result. i don't know what tomorrow looks like yet. i'm closing the laptop and going to bed. yerba mate is warm in the cup beside me. ok.

— last entry. tomorrow i go back to cdmx. roberto will have done something terrible. la polar will have done another saturday at 80db. the rent on the new contract starts december 1. nube will be confused for ten minutes and then act like i never left. camila will have read three more chapters of her detective novel. mom is staying in zapopan with dad. i will call them every other day. the lemon tree dad planted in 2008 will be tall. brown belt me will be back at the gym thursday. ok. closing.

— and: my last memory of being in zapopan as 33-year-old seb in november 2026, before whatever tomorrow's call brings: dad asleep in his chair, mom asleep in her room, lemon tree tall in the dark, cicadas, yerba mate cooling, my old high school posters on the wall above me, camila on whatsapp last-seen 11:24 pm cdmx time, nube probably curled up on her side of the bed, and me, sitting up writing this, and i don't know what tomorrow holds and that's ok. i'm going to sleep now. ok. closing for real.
